_meta.purpose: Short primary-source document cards for the President's Desk Read the Document surface. Excerpts are capped for classroom use; sourceCritique records transcription and locator caveats.
_meta.compiled: D185 M6 home-front politics/economy primary-source lane

categories.label: Confederacy in its own words
categories.blurb: Secession, constitutional, and late-war Confederate documents that openly name slavery, white supremacy, or the contradiction of Black Confederate enlistment.

categories.label: Union war power
categories.blurb: Federal documents that turned emancipation and Black military service into war policy.

categories.label: Black freedom struggle
categories.blurb: Black soldiers and recruiters arguing for freedom, service, pay, and dignity in their own words.

categories.label: Reconstruction & memory
categories.blurb: Postwar documents that show Black citizenship claims, Black Codes, Klan terror, and the making of Civil War memory.

categories.label: Source criticism & under-told voices
categories.blurb: Documents that teach how historians read editions, manuscripts, interview conditions, memory, and viewpoint.

categories.label: Home front, politics & economy
categories.blurb: Election, money, labor, and wartime state-building documents that show the home front as a battlefield.

records.title: Cornerstone Speech
records.author: Alexander H. Stephens
records.verbatimExcerpt: the negro is not equal to the white man; that slavery subordination to the superior race is his natural and normal condition.
records.attribution: Repository: American Battlefield Trust and Teaching American History. Locator: ABT Cornerstone Speech transcript; Teaching American History Corner Stone Speech transcript.
records.sourceCritique: Excerpt uses the shared wording in the two web transcriptions. Teaching American History hyphenates the speech title as Corner Stone; that title variation is outside the quoted excerpt.
records.indictment: The Confederacy's vice president described the new government as founded on Black inequality and slavery.
records.catalystFrame: Before a shot was fired, Stephens told a Savannah audience that slavery and white supremacy were the Confederacy's foundation.

sources.title: Alexander H. Stephens, Cornerstone Speech
Corner Stone Speech

records.title: Mississippi Declaration of Secession
records.author: Mississippi secession convention
records.verbatimExcerpt: Our position is thoroughly identified with the institution of slavery-- the greatest material interest of the world.
records.attribution: Repository: Yale Avalon Project and American Battlefield Trust. Locator: Mississippi declaration, opening paragraph.
records.sourceCritique: The double hyphen and spacing are preserved from the online transcriptions. The document is a public secession declaration, not a later memoir.
records.indictment: Mississippi's own declaration identified slavery as the core interest behind secession.
records.catalystFrame: This is not an outside accusation; it is the state's public explanation for leaving the Union.

sources.title: A Declaration of the Immediate Causes which Induce and Justify the Secession of the State of Mississippi from the Federal Union
Declaration of the Immediate Causes Which Induce and Justify the Secession of South Carolina, Mississippi, Georgia, and Texas

records.title: Confederate Constitution
records.author: Confederate States constitutional convention
records.verbatimExcerpt: No bill of attainder, ex post facto law, or law denying or impairing the right of property in negro slaves shall be passed.
records.attribution: Repository: Yale Avalon Project and National Park Service. Locator: Confederate Constitution, Article I, Section 9, clause 4.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt follows the constitutional clause as transcribed by Avalon and quoted in the NPS Union Dissolved teaching PDF.
records.indictment: The Confederate national charter protected slave property explicitly.
records.catalystFrame: Confederate leaders did not merely tolerate slavery; they wrote protection for property in enslaved people into their constitution.

sources.title: Constitution of the Confederate States; March 11, 1861
Union Dissolved
sources.repository: National Park Service, Appomattox Court House National Historical Park

records.title: Stephen F. Hale to Governor Beriah Magoffin
records.author: Stephen F. Hale
records.verbatimExcerpt: all be degraded to a position of equality with free negroes, stand side by side with them at the polls, and fraternize in all the social relations of life
records.attribution: Repository: Teaching American History, CivilWarCauses.org, and UNC DocSouth. Locator: Hale letter to Kentucky governor Beriah Magoffin, December 27, 1860; OR Series IV, Vol. 1, pp. 4-15.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt starts after Hale's dash so the quoted fragment stays within the word cap while preserving his racial-equality panic language.
records.indictment: A secession commissioner tried to move Kentucky by warning that abolition would produce racial equality.
records.catalystFrame: The argument was not abstract federal theory; Hale appealed directly to white fear of Black citizenship and social equality.

sources.title: Stephen F. Hale to Governor Beriah Magoffin
Stephen F. Hale, Commissioner from Alabama to Kentucky
sources.locator: Transcription from Official Records, Series IV, Vol. 1, pp. 4-15

sources.title: The History and Debates of the Convention of the People of Alabama
sources.locator: William Russell Smith volume, Hale letter transcription

records.title: Howell Cobb to James A. Seddon
records.author: Howell Cobb
records.verbatimExcerpt: If slaves will make good soldiers our whole theory of slavery is wrong
records.attribution: Repository: Encyclopedia Virginia and Official Records. Locator: Cobb letter to Secretary of War James A. Seddon, January 8, 1865; OR Series IV, Vol. 3, pp. 1009-1010.
records.sourceCritique: Encyclopedia Virginia provides the online transcription; the Official Records locator identifies the wartime printed source. The excerpt omits the following hostile prediction to keep the focus on the ideological admission.
records.indictment: Cobb admitted that arming enslaved men would contradict the Confederacy's own theory of slavery.
records.catalystFrame: The late-war manpower crisis exposed the central contradiction: the Confederacy needed soldiers but rested its cause on enslaved people being unfit for citizenship and arms.

sources.title: Letter from Howell Cobb to James A. Seddon
The War of the Rebellion: Official Records

records.title: Emancipation Proclamation
records.author: Abraham Lincoln
records.verbatimExcerpt: such persons of suitable condition, will be received into the armed service of the United States
records.attribution: Repository: National Archives and Yale Avalon Project. Locator: final Emancipation Proclamation, military-service clause.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt is the short military-service clause; the surrounding sentence names forts, positions, stations, vessels, and other service.
records.indictment: The proclamation tied emancipation to Union military power and opened federal service to Black men.

sources.title: Transcript of the Proclamation
The Emancipation Proclamation

records.title: Men of Color, To Arms!
records.author: Frederick Douglass
records.verbatimExcerpt: A war undertaken and brazenly carried on for the perpetual enslavement of colored men, calls logically and loudly for colored men to help suppress it.
records.attribution: Repository: House Divided Project and Teaching American History. Locator: Frederick Douglass, Men of Color, To Arms!, March 21, 1863.
records.sourceCritique: The National Park Service classroom PDF hyphenates 'perpetual' across a line; this card follows continuous web transcriptions from House Divided and Teaching American History.
records.indictment: Douglass framed Black enlistment as a logical answer to a war fought to perpetuate Black enslavement.

sources.title: Frederick Douglass, Men of Color, To Arms!
Men of Color, To Arms!
Colored Men to Arms

records.title: James Henry Gooding's equal-pay letter
records.author: James Henry Gooding
records.verbatimExcerpt: We have done a Soldiers Duty. Why cant we have a Soldiers pay?
records.attribution: Repository: University of Maryland Freedmen and Southern Society Project, National Park Service, and DocsTeach/National Archives. Locator: Corporal James Henry Gooding to Abraham Lincoln, September 28, 1863; NARA Record Group 94.
records.sourceCritique: Spelling and punctuation follow the University of Maryland transcription. NPS quotes normalized snippets; DocsTeach supplies the National Archives locator for the original letter.
records.indictment: Gooding demanded equal pay by grounding Black soldiers' claim in their actual service and sacrifice.

sources.title: Massachusetts Black Corporal to the President
sources.repository: University of Maryland, Freedmen and Southern Society Project
sources.locator: James Henry Gooding to Abraham Lincoln, September 28, 1863

sources.title: Corporal James H. Gooding
sources.locator: Biographical page with equal-pay letter excerpts

sources.title: James Henry Gooding, Black Soldier's Appeal to Lincoln for Equal Pay
sources.locator: Record Group 94, Records of the Adjutant General's Office

records.title: Mississippi Apprentice Law (Black Code)
records.author: Mississippi legislature
records.verbatimExcerpt: the former owner of said minors shall have the preference, when in the opinion of the court, he or she shall be a suitable person for that purpose.
records.attribution: Repository: University of Nebraska Teaching Legal History and Tennessee State Library & Archives. Locator: Mississippi Black Codes, Chapter V, apprenticeship law, section 1, approved November 22, 1865.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt follows the University of Nebraska transcription; the Tennessee State Library PDF independently carries the same former-owner preference clause. The law called the relationship apprenticeship, but it handed courts power to bind Black children to former enslavers.
records.indictment: Mississippi's Black Code tried to restore planter control over Black children immediately after formal emancipation.

sources.title: Mississippi Black Codes (1865)
sources.repository: University of Nebraska Teaching Legal History

sources.title: Mississippi's Black Codes
sources.locator: Teaching PDF, Chapter V apprenticeship excerpt

records.title: Elias Hill before the Joint Select Committee
records.author: Elias Hill
records.verbatimExcerpt: They pointed pistols at me all around my head once or twice, as if they were going to shoot me, telling me they were going to kill me
records.attribution: Repository: Digital History and U.S. Congress/Internet Archive. Locator: Report of the Joint Select Committee to Inquire into the Condition of Affairs in the Late Insurrectionary States, South Carolina testimony, Elias Hill, July 25, 1871, p. 1408.
records.sourceCritique: Digital History supplies a readable excerpt; the congressional report page images/OCR locate Hill's sworn testimony in the 1872 South Carolina volume. Ellipses in classroom excerpts mark omitted testimony, not a different witness.
records.indictment: Hill's sworn testimony shows Reconstruction terror as political violence aimed at Black citizenship, schools, preaching, and Republican organizing.

sources.title: Elias Hill Testimony
sources.locator: Excerpt from Joint Select Committee report, South Carolina testimony

sources.title: Ku Klux Klan Congressional Testimony
sources.locator: South Carolina Volume 1, Elias Hill testimony, p. 1408

records.title: What the Black Man Wants
records.author: Frederick Douglass
records.verbatimExcerpt: I am for the “immediate, unconditional, and universal” enfranchisement of the black man, in every state in the Union.
records.attribution: Repository: Teaching American History and Library of Congress. Locator: Frederick Douglass, What the Black Man Wants, January 26, 1865; The Equality of All Men before the Law, LOC item mfd.22014.
records.sourceCritique: Teaching American History dates the address January 26, 1865 and points to the Library of Congress pamphlet. Some NPS teaching media excerpt the same Reconstruction suffrage argument separately, so this card follows the TAH/LOC dating and locator.
records.indictment: Douglass argued before Appomattox that emancipation without Black suffrage would leave freedom vulnerable to mob rule and political betrayal.

sources.title: What the Black Man Wants
The Equality of All Men before the Law

records.title: Gettysburg Address, Bliss copy
records.author: Abraham Lincoln
records.date: November 19, 1863; later copy for Alexander Bliss
records.verbatimExcerpt: government of the people, by the people, for the people, shall not perish from the earth.
records.attribution: Repository: Library of Congress and National Park Service. Locator: LOC Gettysburg Address exhibition; NPS Lincoln Memorial Gettysburg Address / Bliss-copy note.
records.sourceCritique: There are five known manuscript copies, with small wording and punctuation differences. The Bliss copy became the standard inscription text, so a citation must name which copy is being quoted.
records.indictment: Even iconic wartime language has a manuscript history; classroom quotation should not pretend there is only one undifferentiated text.

sources.title: Gettysburg Address Exhibition
sources.locator: Exhibition overview naming the five manuscript copies and repositories

sources.title: Gettysburg Address

records.title: Susie King Taylor, Thoughts on Present Conditions
records.author: Susie King Taylor
records.verbatimExcerpt: Was the war in vain? Has it brought freedom, in the full sense of the word, or has it not made our condition more hopeless?
records.attribution: Repository: UNC Documenting the American South and Internet Archive. Locator: Reminiscences of My Life in Camp, chapter XIII, Thoughts on Present Conditions, p. 61.
records.sourceCritique: Taylor wrote decades after wartime service, but as a participant. DocSouth records its transcription normalizations, so the card treats the memoir as a remembered first-person source, not a stenographic wartime diary.
records.indictment: Taylor connected Union victory to the unfinished fight against lynching, racial terror, and unequal citizenship.

sources.title: Reminiscences of My Life in Camp
sources.locator: Chapter XIII, Thoughts on Present Conditions, p. 61

sources.title: Reminiscences of My Life in Camp
After the War
sources.locator: Historic Document Library excerpt from Taylor, chapter XIII

records.title: Dolly Lunt Burge on Sherman's March
records.author: Dolly Sumner Lunt Burge
records.verbatimExcerpt: There go my mules, my sheep, and, worse than all, my boys [slaves]!
records.attribution: Repository: UNC Documenting the American South and Digital Library of Georgia. Locator: A Woman's Wartime Journal, November 19, 1864, p. 24.
records.sourceCritique: The diary was published in 1918 with an introduction and notes. The bracketed [slaves] gloss is part of the edited text; the source preserves an enslaver's viewpoint and must be framed as such.
records.indictment: The hard-war eyewitness record shows real civilian fear while also exposing enslaver language that treated people as property.

sources.title: A Woman's Wartime Journal
A Woman's Wartime Journal
sources.locator: North American Slave Narratives metadata record

records.title: William McCarter's Irish Brigade memoir
records.author: William McCarter
records.date: Postwar memoir; Fredericksburg memory of December 13, 1862
records.verbatimExcerpt: death, havoc, and carnage was visible at every step on the ground fronting Marye's Heights.
records.attribution: Repository: National Museum of Civil War Medicine, University of Alabama bibliography, and National Park Service. Locator: McCarter, My Life in the Irish Brigade, p. 181; NPS Fredericksburg Union OOB.
records.sourceCritique: McCarter was an Irish immigrant private in the 116th Pennsylvania, but this is a postwar memoir edited for publication, not a letter written on the field. The card uses it as veteran memory with source limits.
records.indictment: Immigrant soldier testimony belongs in the war story without turning the Irish Brigade into a simple romance of ethnic valor.

sources.title: The Medical Treatment of Private William McCarter
sources.repository: National Museum of Civil War Medicine
sources.locator: Quotes My Life in the Irish Brigade, p. 181, and identifies McCarter's 116th Pennsylvania service

sources.title: McCarter, William. My Life in the Irish Brigade
sources.locator: Published memoir bibliography and index, Savas Publishing 1996

sources.title: Order of Battle Fredericksburg Union Right Grand Division
sources.locator: Second Corps / First Division / Second Brigade, Irish Brigade

records.title: William Dwyer's Irish Brigade letter
records.author: William Dwyer
records.verbatimExcerpt: we were kept back and would not be let go in account of we being Irish.
records.attribution: Repository: Irish in the American Civil War / National Archives pension-file transcript. Locator: William Dwyer Dependent Mother's Pension File WC103233; letter of January 23, 1863.
records.sourceCritique: The public transcript identifies Dwyer as a Tipperary native in Co. H, 63rd New York, Irish Brigade, and notes that punctuation was added for modern reading. The letter is a soldier-family source preserved in a dependent mother's pension file, not an official report.
records.indictment: Dwyer turned the Irish Brigade's Fredericksburg losses into a charge of ethnic mistreatment, showing immigrant service and resentment in the same voice.

sources.title: In Account Of We Being Irish: A New Irish Brigade Letter After Fredericksburg
sources.repository: Irish in the American Civil War
sources.locator: William Dwyer Dependent Mother's Pension File WC103233, January 23, 1863 letter

sources.title: Archived transcript: In Account Of We Being Irish
sources.repository: RSSing mirror of Irish in the American Civil War
sources.locator: Lines for William Dwyer's January 23, 1863 letter

sources.title: Irish Soldiers in the Union Army
sources.locator: Context for Irish enlistment, remittances, and Irish Brigade leadership

records.title: Blind Memorandum
records.author: Abraham Lincoln
records.verbatimExcerpt: This morning, as for some days past, it seems exceedingly probable that this Administration will not be re-elected. Then it will be my duty to so co-operate with the President elect, as to save the Union between the election and the inauguration;
records.attribution: Repository: Library of Congress and House Divided / Dickinson College. Locator: LOC Abraham Lincoln Papers item mal4359700; House Divided Blind Memorandum transcript, August 23, 1864.
records.sourceCritique: The LOC item supplies the public-domain manuscript and House Divided gives a classroom transcription; the excerpt preserves the shared wording, including "co-operate" and "President elect." It stops before Lincoln's final can-not-save-it clause to stay under the card cap; later teaching pages often modernize these forms.
records.indictment: The 1864 election was not a decorative clock event; Lincoln privately prepared to cooperate with a peace-platform victor to save the Union before inauguration.

sources.title: Abraham Lincoln Papers: Blind Memorandum
Abraham Lincoln, Blind Memorandum

records.title: First Legal Tender Act
records.author: Thirty-Seventh Congress
records.verbatimExcerpt: shall also be lawful money and a legal tender in payment of all debts, public and private, within the United States
records.attribution: Repository: U.S. Statutes at Large / govinfo and FRASER. Locator: 12 Stat. 345, ch. 33, February 25, 1862, legal-tender clause; Spaulding, History of the Legal Tender Paper Money, reprinted statutory text.
records.sourceCritique: The excerpt isolates the shared legal-tender clause. The statute immediately excepted duties on imports and interest on federal bonds/notes, which stayed payable in coin; the card names that limit rather than turning greenbacks into unrestricted hard money.
records.indictment: Congress made wartime paper money a legal instrument of national power, while preserving gold channels for tariff and bond credibility.

sources.title: An Act to authorize the Issue of United States Notes
History of the Legal Tender Paper Money issued during the Great Rebellion
sources.repository: FRASER, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
sources.locator: Reprinted statutory text of the Legal Tender Act
